class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Database Systems in Healthcare
learning_objectives: Explain why databases are essential infrastructure in healthcare
Differentiate between relational, document, and graph database models
Identify common healthcare databases and their purposes
readings: Database Systems: Design, Implementation, and Management Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Every healthcare system relies on databases to store patient records, manage billing, track inventory, and support analytics. This course builds your ability to design, query, and manage healthcare databases.
key_concepts: Database models: relational (SQL), document (NoSQL), graph, time-series
Healthcare database examples: EHR backend, claims databases, registries, MPI
ACID properties and why they matter for patient data
Database Management System (DBMS) landscape: PostgreSQL, SQL Server, MongoDB, FHIR stores
Data integrity constraints in clinical contexts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploration of a sample healthcare database schema showing patients, encounters, diagnoses, medications, and providers
setup_requirements: PostgreSQL installed or cloud database access
SQL client tool (pgAdmin, DBeaver, or similar)

review.value: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability - guarantees ensuring database transactions are processed reliably, critical for patient safety
Data organized in tables with rows and columns, connected by foreign keys - dominant model for structured healthcare data

learning_objectives: Apply entity-relationship modeling to healthcare scenarios
Normalize database tables through third normal form
Design primary and foreign key relationships for clinical data

lecture_notes.introduction: Good database design prevents data anomalies, ensures integrity, and supports efficient queries. We learn to model healthcare data correctly from the start.
key_concepts: Entity-relationship (ER) diagrams for healthcare domains
Entities: Patient, Provider, Encounter, Diagnosis, Medication, Procedure
Normalization: 1NF (atomic values), 2NF (full functional dependency), 3NF (no transitive dependency)
De-normalization tradeoffs for query performance
Healthcare-specific modeling: handling multiple diagnoses per encounter, medication history
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design an ER diagram for a clinic database handling patients, appointments, providers, diagnoses, and prescriptions

review.value: Every non-key attribute depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key - eliminates update anomalies
Patients have many encounters, encounters have many diagnoses - resolved with junction tables like encounter_diagnosis

learning_objectives: Write SQL SELECT queries to retrieve clinical data
Use WHERE, JOIN, and GROUP BY to answer healthcare questions
Apply aggregate functions to compute clinical metrics

lecture_notes.introduction: SQL is the universal language for querying relational databases. Healthcare analysts use SQL daily to extract data for quality reporting, research, and operational decisions.
key_concepts: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY for basic data retrieval
JOIN types: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT for combining related tables
GROUP BY and aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG for clinical metrics
Filtering with dates for encounter-based queries
Subqueries and common table expressions for complex analytics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write queries against the sample healthcare database: find diabetic patients, calculate average length of stay, list top diagnoses by frequency
setup_requirements: PostgreSQL with sample healthcare database loaded

review.value: SQL operation combining rows from two tables based on a related column, essential for connecting patients to their encounters and diagnoses
Groups rows sharing values in specified columns, enabling aggregate calculations like average length of stay per diagnosis

class_schedule.topic: Advanced SQL and Clinical Quality Queries
learning_objectives: Write advanced SQL with window functions and CTEs
Implement clinical quality measure logic in SQL
Optimize queries for large healthcare datasets

lecture_notes.introduction: Real healthcare analytics requires advanced SQL. Today we implement actual clinical quality measures as SQL queries and learn optimization techniques for million-row datasets.
key_concepts: Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD for temporal clinical analysis
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) for readable complex queries
Implementing eCQM logic: numerator, denominator, exclusions in SQL
Query execution plans and index optimization
Date arithmetic for clinical timeframes (30-day readmission windows)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a 30-day hospital readmission measure in SQL: define the denominator (index admissions), numerator (readmissions within 30 days), and exclusions

review.value: Functions operating across a set of rows related to the current row - essential for comparing sequential encounters or calculating running totals
Translating clinical quality measure specifications into SQL queries that identify patient populations meeting measure criteria

learning_objectives: Describe the architecture of a healthcare data warehouse
Differentiate between OLTP and OLAP systems
Design a star schema for clinical analytics
readings: Healthcare Data Warehousing: Design and Implementation

lecture_notes.introduction: Data warehouses aggregate data from multiple source systems into an analytics-optimized structure. They are the foundation of healthcare business intelligence and population health platforms.
key_concepts: OLTP vs. OLAP: transactional vs. analytical workloads
ETL pipeline: extract from EHR/claims, transform/clean, load into warehouse
Star schema: fact tables (encounters, claims) and dimension tables (patient, provider, date, diagnosis)
Slowly changing dimensions for tracking patient demographics over time
Data lake vs. data warehouse in healthcare
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a star schema for a hospital readmission analytics warehouse, then write dimensional queries against it

review.value: Dimensional model with a central fact table linked to surrounding dimension tables, optimized for analytical queries
Extract, Transform, Load - pipeline that moves data from operational systems into the warehouse after cleaning and standardization

class_schedule.topic: Database Security and HIPAA Technical Safeguards
learning_objectives: Implement database security controls required by HIPAA
Design role-based access control for clinical databases
Understand database auditing and monitoring for compliance
readings: HIPAA Security Rule Technical Safeguard Requirements

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare databases contain the most sensitive data in any industry. Technical safeguards mandated by HIPAA must be implemented at the database level to protect patient privacy.
key_concepts: Role-based access control (RBAC) for clinical databases
Encryption at rest (TDE) and in transit (TLS)
Database audit logging: who accessed what, when, and why
Row-level security for multi-tenant healthcare applications
Backup, recovery, and business continuity for healthcare data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement RBAC in PostgreSQL: create roles for clinician, nurse, coder, and analyst with appropriate permissions

review.value: Access control model where permissions are assigned to roles (physician, nurse, coder) rather than individuals, simplifying PHI access management
Recording all data access events to detect unauthorized access and support HIPAA breach investigation requirements

questions.question: A clinic needs to track patients, their appointments with providers, and diagnoses assigned during each appointment. A patient can have many appointments, each appointment is with one provider, and multiple diagnoses can be assigned per appointment. Draw the entity-relationship model (list entities, attributes, and relationships) and explain your normalization decisions.
questions.answer: Entities: Patient (patient_id PK, name, dob, gender, phone), Provider (provider_id PK, name, specialty, npi), Appointment (appointment_id PK, patient_id FK, provider_id FK, date, time, reason), Diagnosis (diagnosis_id PK, icd10_code, description), Appointment_Diagnosis (appointment_id FK, diagnosis_id FK, sequence_number) - junction table. Relationships: Patient 1:M Appointment, Provider 1:M Appointment, Appointment M:M Diagnosis (via junction). This is in 3NF because all non-key attributes depend only on their table's primary key with no transitive dependencies.

rubric.key: Proper relationship cardinalities and junction table

questions.question: Write a SQL query to find all patients diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes (ICD-10 codes starting with E11) who had more than 3 encounters in 2023. Return patient name, total encounter count, and most recent encounter date.
questions.answer: SELECT p.name, COUNT(DISTINCT e.encounter_id) AS encounter_count, MAX(e.encounter_date) AS last_encounter FROM patient p JOIN encounter e ON p.patient_id = e.patient_id JOIN encounter_diagnosis ed ON e.encounter_id = ed.encounter_id JOIN diagnosis d ON ed.diagnosis_id = d.diagnosis_id WHERE d.icd10_code LIKE 'E11%' AND e.encounter_date BETWEEN '2023-01-01' AND '2023-12-31' GROUP BY p.patient_id, p.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.encounter_id) > 3 ORDER BY encounter_count DESC;

rubric.key: Correct JOINs connecting all required tables
Proper WHERE filtering for ICD-10 and date range
Correct GROUP BY and HAVING for encounter count threshold

questions.question: Explain the difference between OLTP and OLAP databases in a healthcare context. Give a specific example of each and explain why they require different architectures.
questions.answer: OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) handles real-time operational workloads with frequent small reads and writes. Example: the EHR production database that records patient registrations, clinical notes, and orders in real time. It requires normalized schemas for data integrity, row-level locking, and fast individual record access. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) handles complex analytical queries across large datasets. Example: a population health data warehouse aggregating data from multiple hospitals to calculate quality measures across millions of encounters. It requires denormalized star schemas for fast aggregation, columnar storage for scan performance, and batch loading from source systems.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of OLTP and OLAP

questions.question: Write a SQL query using a window function to identify hospital readmissions within 30 days. For each patient admission, determine if there was a subsequent admission within 30 days of discharge.
questions.answer: WITH ordered_admissions AS (SELECT patient_id, encounter_id, admission_date, discharge_date, LEAD(admission_date) OVER (PARTITION BY patient_id ORDER BY admission_date) AS next_admission_date FROM encounter WHERE encounter_type = 'inpatient') SELECT patient_id, encounter_id AS index_encounter, admission_date, discharge_date, next_admission_date, next_admission_date - discharge_date AS days_to_readmission FROM ordered_admissions WHERE next_admission_date IS NOT NULL AND next_admission_date - discharge_date <= 30;

rubric.key: Correct use of LEAD window function with PARTITION BY patient
Proper 30-day calculation from discharge to next admission

major_assignments.title: Healthcare Database Design and Implementation
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a relational database for a multi-specialty clinic, including schema design, sample data loading, SQL queries for clinical and operational reporting, and security implementation.
objectives: Apply database design principles to a healthcare scenario
Implement a normalized schema in PostgreSQL
Write complex SQL queries for clinical reporting
deliverables: SQL DDL scripts for table creation
Sample data loading scripts (minimum 100 patients, 500 encounters)

criteria: Complete ER diagram with all relationships
Range of complexity from basic to advanced
Proper use of JOINs, aggregations, and window functions
Well-designed normalized schema appropriate for healthcare
Complex and clinically relevant SQL queries
Thorough documentation of design and implementation

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of database concepts, SQL skills, and healthcare data modeling

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on SQL programming exercises and database administration tasks

major_assignments.description: Healthcare database design and implementation project

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of database design and SQL fundamentals

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including data warehousing and security topics

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in grade reduction. Lecture attendance is strongly encouraged.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. SQL lab submissions must be completed within one week of the lab session.
course_policies.collaboration: Students may discuss concepts but all SQL code must be individually written. Identical query submissions will be treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All database designs and SQL queries must be original work. Use of AI code generation tools must be disclosed and the student must explain the code.
course_policies.technology_requirements: PostgreSQL installation (local or cloud), SQL client tool, ER diagramming software. Lab computers are available with pre-configured environments.
